text.title: Engagement Pattern Memory — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for maintaining a per-engagement memory layer that prevents cross-client convention leak and re-learn cost.

rule.statement: Each active engagement MUST have a dedicated memory file at a fixed, isolated location (e.g., `~/engagements//memory.md`). Conventions, reviewer preferences, glossary terms, traps, and deploy quirks are scoped to that file. Forbidden: shared "tips for all clients" file that mixes engagement-specific knowledge.
rule.rationale: Shared memory leaks: a reviewer-preference for Client A ("prefers shorter PR descriptions") leaks into Client B and gets you flagged for missing detail. Per-engagement scope prevents this at the file level.

rule.statement: At the end of each working session, the contractor MUST spend 10-15 min updating the engagement memory: new repo conventions observed, new reviewer feedback, deploy quirks encountered, traps hit, glossary terms learned. Write happens at session end, NOT in the next session — by then the patterns have decayed.

rationale.source: Memory decay research, GTD ubiquitous-capture practice

rule.rationale: Write delay loses 40-60% of session-specific learnings within 24h. Session-end write captures while memory is fresh. The 10-15 min cost pays back many-fold by avoiding re-learn next session.

rule.statement: Engagement glossary terms MUST live in the per-engagement file, not in a global glossary. The same word ("customer", "active", "shipped") often means different things across engagements. The engagement memory's glossary section is the source of truth for THIS engagement.

rationale.source: DDD bounded-context per project, glossary-leakage research

rule.rationale: Glossary leak is the most expensive cross-engagement memory failure — using the wrong meaning of "shipped" once produces a customer-visible defect. Per-engagement isolation matches the bounded-context pattern.

rule.statement: Quarterly, the contractor runs scripts/decay-cleanup.py to flag entries older than 90 days with no recent reference. Stale entries are either: kept with explicit "still current" annotation, retired (moved to archive section), or deleted. Forbidden: indefinite append-only memory that grows without prune.

rationale.source: Information hygiene research, GTD review cadence

rule.rationale: Without prune, the file grows to 10k+ tokens of mostly-stale content; loading it at session start becomes counterproductive. Quarterly prune keeps the memory current and load-efficient.

rule.statement: The agent loaded for engagement A MUST NOT have engagement B's memory in its context. Session-start hook loads ONLY the active engagement's file. If contractor mid-session realizes they're in the wrong engagement context, they restart the session — they do NOT continue with mixed context.
rule.rationale: Mixed context leads to recommending Client A's conventions on Client B's code. The cost of restart (a few minutes) is far less than the cost of cross-leak (PR rework, reviewer trust loss).

example.title: Engagement memory file structure + session-end update
example.code:
~/engagements/acme-corp/memory.md --- Last updated: 2026-05-19 Active since: 2026-02-01 ---
## Repo conventions - Branch naming: feature/TICKET-NNN-short-desc - Commits: imperative voice, max 72 chars, no body required - TS strict mode; no `any`; explicit return types on exported functions - Tests colocated: foo.ts next to foo.test.ts - ESLint config: `.eslintrc.json` extends @acme/eslint-config-strict
## Reviewer preferences ### Maria (Tech Lead) - Prefers SHORT PR descriptions (1-line summary + bullets) - Strict on test coverage for new logic (asks for tests if missing) - Pings on Slack if PR sits > 24h
### Diego (Senior Eng) - Likes architectural ADR links in PR if change touches /core - OK with longer PRs but expects clear commit history
## Deploy quirks - Staging auto-deploys main branch - Production: manual via /ops/deploy.sh; requires Maria's approval token - Rollback: not push -f; revert PR + redeploy
## Recurring traps - 2026-04-12: tried to use `Promise.allSettled` — codebase is Node 14, only `Promise.all` supported. (FIXED by upgrade ticket #1245) - 2026-05-03: assumed user_id was UUID; it's bigint per legacy schema. Glossary updated.
## Glossary (this engagement only) - "active customer" = billing_status IN ('active', 'past_due'); last_login < 60d - "shipped" = deployed to production AND post-deploy smoke-tests passed; NOT just merged - "platform" = monorepo top-level; NOT a tier of customer
## Resolved-q-log - 2026-05-12: "Can I use new ESM-only deps?" — Maria: yes, but pin versions explicitly.
## Session-end (2026-05-19) - Added trap: tried to use @acme/utils v2; tree-shaking issue. Use v1.x. - Diego asked for ADR on new caching strategy — drafted in /docs/adr-2026-014.
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5: per-engagement file, session-end write, glossary isolated, structured sections.

example.title: Shared scratch file across clients
example.code:
~/notes/dev-tips.md (one file for all clients) - "Use Promise.allSettled" (works on most, NOT acme-corp Node 14) - "Reviewer prefers detailed PRs" (TRUE for client-X, FALSE for Maria @ acme) - "Active customer = ..." (definition from client-X, WRONG for acme)
Outcome: contractor pastes "active customer" SQL from notes into acme codebase; PR fails review because acme's definition is different.
Repair: enforce r1, r3. Per-engagement file; per-engagement glossary. Shared notes are for tooling / general dev tricks only, NEVER client-specifics.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r3, r5 violations — the classic single-notes-file leak.
